Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: De Cruz Andrea Heidi

Facts.Text: The consumer, a television actress, learned about the slimming pills through fellow actors and promotional materials.
She procured a supply of the slimming pills through a colleague rather than purchasing them directly from an authorized retail pharmacy.
She consumed the pills for several weeks to manage her weight.
She suffered severe liver failure requiring an emergency liver transplant after consuming the pills.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a consumer's failure to purchase a product from an authorized retailer breaks the chain of causation in a product liability claim against the distributor.
Party_Details.Rule: A consumer's method of procurement does not break the chain of causation if the distributor actively marketed the product as universally safe and the procurement method did not introduce new risks.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff stated she relied on the distributor's heavy advertising campaigns assuring the product was 100% safe. The Defendant argued she assumed the risk by buying the pills through an unofficial channel.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the consumer's reliance on the distributor's mass advertising was reasonable. The fact that she obtained the pills through a colleague rather than a pharmacy did not break the chain of causation because the pills themselves were inherently toxic.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: TV Media Pte Ltd
Party_Details.Law_Firm: B Rao and K S Rajah

Facts.Text: The company entered into an exclusive distributorship agreement to market and sell a brand of imported slimming pills.
The company launched an aggressive mass-media advertising campaign featuring celebrity endorsements claiming the product was 100% safe.
The company did not conduct any independent laboratory testing to verify the ingredients of the pills before distributing them.
The company relied entirely on the importer's assurances and the health authority's standard import documentation regarding product safety.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a sole distributor is legally responsible in tort to consumers for failing to independently test a product that it aggressively advertises as completely safe.
Party_Details.Rule: A sole distributor who actively promotes a product and makes specific safety claims assumes a duty of care to consumers and cannot blindly rely on the importer's assurances without conducting independent verification.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant argued it was merely a distributor and was entitled to rely on the importer and regulatory authorities for safety checks. The Plaintiff stated that by acting as the sole promoter and expressly guaranteeing safety, the distributor assumed a primary duty to verify those claims.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal dismissed the distributor's appeal. It found that TV Media was not a passive wholesaler; it aggressively marketed the pills and endorsed their safety. By doing so, it assumed a duty of care to consumers and was negligent in failing to conduct its own independent safety tests.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: De Cruz Andrea Heidi

Facts.Text: She consumed the imported slimming pills for several weeks to manage her weight.
She suffered severe liver failure requiring an emergency liver transplant due to undeclared toxic ingredients in the pills.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether a consumer can pierce the corporate veil to hold an importing company's director personally responsible for product injuries.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporate director can be held personally liable for the torts of the company if they expressly directed, authorized, or procured the commission of the tortious act.
Party_Details.Application: The Plaintiff stated the director personally orchestrated the importation of the toxic pills without ensuring regulatory compliance. The Defendant argued he was protected by the corporate veil and acted only in his capacity as a corporate officer.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court found that the director personally procured and directed the negligent importation of the pills, rendering him personally liable as a joint tortfeasor alongside his company.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Woo Bih Li J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Semon

Facts.Text: He was the controlling director and guiding mind of the company that imported the slimming pills.
He personally submitted import documentation to the health authorities omitting the existence of toxic ingredients in the pills.
He authorized the release of the pills to the sole distributor without establishing any system for batch testing or quality control.
He assured the distributor that the pills were entirely safe and compliant with local health regulations.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the controlling director of an importing company is personally liable in negligence for authorizing the distribution of a toxic product.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors are not automatically liable for a company's negligence, but personal liability attaches if the director was personally involved in directing or procuring the specific negligent acts.
Party_Details.Application: The Defendant argued that any negligence was committed by the company, and he was shielded by corporate limited liability. The Plaintiff stated the Defendant was the sole decision-maker who actively drove the negligent importation process.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The Court of Appeal dismissed the director's appeal. It ruled that his personal, hands-on involvement in importing the pills, submitting regulatory documents, and failing to implement safety checks meant he had expressly directed the negligence, justifying personal liability.
